content:
As Director of Strategic Operations, I assess that our current campaign has secured key logistical corridors but is falling short of decisive momentum against enemy command nodes. We must shift emphasis to precision interdiction of high-value targets using SOF-aviation task forces to disrupt adversary decision-making.

I recommend re-tasking two battalions from static area control duties to mobile strike operations within Sector Gamma by 15 October, supported by reallocated ISR assets.

Key risk: Overextension of forward logistics chains could undermine operational tempo if enemy forces conduct coordinated counter-mobility strikes.

[Deputy Director Elena Rostova - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with General Krasnov’s assessment—our gains in mobility and supply security are necessary but insufficient without degrading enemy C2 capacity. Precision strikes must be synchronized with electronic warfare to suppress adversary communications during operations.

I recommend establishing a joint targeting cell integrating SOF, signals intelligence, and air assets under a single operational chain by 5 October to ensure rapid target nomination and strike coordination.

Key concern: Without tighter integration, we risk fratricide or missed windows due to delayed sensor-to-shooter loops.

[Director of Logistics and Sustainment - MAJOR_POWER]: I support the shift toward mobile strike operations, but current supply infrastructure in Sector Gamma cannot sustain increased SOF and aviation activity without significant reinforcement. Pre-positioning fuel, ammunition, and maintenance packages at forward operating points is essential before re-tasking forces.

I recommend accelerating the deployment of two mobile logistics support teams to Sector Gamma by 1 October, concurrent with the establishment of protected airhead nodes to enable responsive resupply.

Key risk: Without secured forward sustainment, high-tempo strikes will degrade within 72 hours due to attrition in aviation assets and SOF endurance limits.

[Colonel Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I endorse the shift to precision interdiction and agree with the need for integrated targeting and logistics enablement. However, we must condition force re-tasking on secure airhead establishment—forward operations cannot succeed if aviation remains vulnerable to enemy air defenses.

I recommend that the mobile logistics teams deploy alongside a dedicated SEAD/DEAD package to neutralize known IADS in Sector Gamma’s approach corridors by 25 September, ensuring air superiority prior to SOF and aviation movement.

Key risk: Premature force repositioning without suppression of enemy air defenses will lead to unacceptable platform losses and mission failure.

[Deputy Director Tarasov - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I align with Colonel Morozov’s conditionality: SEAD/DEAD enforcement must precede logistics and SOF deployment. Without assured air access, both sustainment and strike operations are untenable.

I recommend tasking the 4th Air Regiment with a focused IADS degradation campaign from 15–25 September, using SEAD-optimized platforms and decoy operations to map and destroy high-priority threats in Sector Gamma’s western and central approach zones.

Key concern: Adversary integration of low-frequency radar and mobile SAMs poses a dynamic threat; failure to conduct real-time ELINT-driven targeting during the SEAD window will leave critical nodes intact.

[General Dmitri Sokolov - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I concur with the sequencing: SEAD/DEAD must precede logistics emplacement and strike operations. The 4th Air Regiment’s campaign should be synchronized with electronic warfare assets to exploit adversary radar activation patterns identified by ELINT.

I direct the 4th Air Regiment, in coordination with GRU EW units, to execute the IADS degradation campaign on schedule (15–25 September), with real-time targeting feeds integrated into strike packages; mobile logistics teams will move no earlier than 26 September, contingent on confirmation of air corridor security by Joint Operations Command.

Key risk: Over-reliance on pre-planned SEAD strikes without adaptive ELINT feedback loops could result in residual IADS survivability, endangering follow-on forces.

[Deputy Director Elena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I support the established sequencing and General Sokolov’s directive: SEAD/DEAD operations must be dynamically informed by GRU EW and SIGINT assets to ensure target set currency. Precision interdiction is only as effective as the intelligence that enables it.

I recommend instituting a daily ELINT fusion cell at Joint Operations Command starting 10 September, integrating GRU, Air Force, and Signals Intelligence Directorate analysts to produce time-sensitive target packages and adjust SEAD planning in response to adversary IADS repositioning.

Key risk: Fragmented intelligence sharing between operational and technical units could delay strike targeting, creating windows for enemy radar reactivation and jeopardizing air corridor integrity.

[Colonel Irina Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  
I endorse Minister Volkov’s emphasis on dynamic targeting and the establishment of the ELINT fusion cell—this is critical to counter adversary IADS mobility. Real-time intelligence integration is not optional; it is the linchpin of effective SEAD.

I recommend that the fusion cell be granted direct tasking authority over tactical SIGINT platforms and UAV reconnaissance assets operating near Sector Gamma, ensuring priority collection on radar emission signatures and SAM displacement patterns.

Key concern: Delays in cross-directorate clearance for reconnaissance asset re-tasking could degrade targeting tempo; I propose streamlining approval to the fusion cell itself, under OPLAN 45-Red protocols, to maintain operational agility.

[Deputy Director Alexei Rozenkov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I align with Dr. Petrova’s assessment: the fusion cell must have operational authority over reconnaissance assets to maintain targeting continuity. Intelligence delays equate to lost windows in SEAD execution.

I direct that OPLAN 45-Red be activated immediately, granting the ELINT fusion cell tier-one priority for UAV and SIGINT platform tasking in Sector Gamma, with daily mission updates reportable directly to Joint Operations Command.

Key risk: Centralizing tasking authority may strain inter-service coordination with Air Force ISR commanders; I recommend assigning liaison officers from the 4th Air Regiment to the fusion cell to ensure synchronization and prevent deconfliction failures.

[General Mikhail Sokolov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

The operational integrity of our SEAD campaign hinges on real-time intelligence synchronization and unimpeded targeting cycles. I approve the activation of OPLAN 45-Red and the赋予 of direct tasking authority to the ELINT fusion cell, as recommended.

I direct that the 4th Air Regiment deploy two embedded liaison officers to the fusion cell by 0800 10 September, with full access to air tasking order (ATO) systems, to ensure seamless deconfliction and coordination between strike packages and ISR collection.

Key risk: Over-reliance on SIGINT-derived targeting without cross-validation from HUMINT or imagery intelligence (IMINT) could lead to target misidentification, particularly in mixed-use terrain near the western corridor—require all high-value strikes to undergo multi-source verification prior to clearance.

As Head of Strategy, I believe continued resistance undermines our leverage and isolates us from shaping the final outcome. We should shift to active negotiation, using our bloc's cohesion to extract concrete concessions on enforcement mechanisms and equity provisions.

Recommended course: Initiate direct talks with swing states this week to co-draft a compromise text that preserves core climate goals while addressing implementation timelines.

Key risk: If we pivot too quickly without clear red-line safeguards, we risk legitimizing weaker commitments and losing credibility with civil society and vulnerable member states.

[Foreign Minister Rafael Durán - SMALL_POWER]: President Marchetti makes a valid point about leverage, but I caution that our credibility *depends* on resistance at this stage—our moral authority is our primary strategic asset. Swing states respect resolve, not early concession.

Recommended course: Maintain public resistance while launching quiet, conditional exploratory talks—frame any compromise as a *reciprocal* de-escalation, not unilateral accommodation.

Key risk: Perceived capitulation now will fracture our coalition; several island states have explicitly warned that support for negotiations without verified ambition benchmarks would trigger withdrawal from the bloc.

[Climate Envoy Dr. Amara Nkosi - SMALL_POWER]: I align with Foreign Minister Durán on preserving our moral authority, but Strategy’s push for engagement cannot be deferred. Our current stance risks being perceived as obstructionist rather than principled—particularly among emerging economies whose buy-in we need.

Recommended course: Publicly reaffirm our red lines on 1.5°C alignment and loss-and-damage financing, while authorizing backchannel talks with the ASEAN and African negotiating groups to co-design ambition-verification protocols that we can champion as non-negotiable in any text.

Key risk: If we maintain rigid public resistance without offering constructive alternatives, we lose influence to the G77-QUAD axis now shaping the middle ground—and may be sidelined in closed-room deals that exclude our core priorities.

[Director Lars Fisker - SMALL_POWER]: I support Envoy Nkosi’s approach: we must preserve our principled stance while injecting strategic pragmatism. Public resistance without a visible pathway to engagement risks ceding agenda-setting power to larger blocs who will define “compromise” on their terms.

Recommended course: Immediately task our technical team to finalize a draft ambition-verification protocol—co-developed with ASEAN and African group focal points—and table it as a formal working paper within 72 hours to position us as solution-providers, not just holdouts.

Key risk: Delaying tangible proposals feeds the narrative that small powers are reactive, not constructive, which weakens our claim to equitable representation in final decision-making.

[Deputy Minister Anika Rao - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with Director Fisker—our leverage lies in being both principled and proactive. We cannot afford to be seen as obstructionist, but nor can we dilute our core demands; the 1.5°C threshold and mandatory loss-and-damage financing must remain non-negotiable pillars.

Recommended course: Release a public statement reaffirming our red lines within 24 hours, simultaneously circulating the draft verification protocol as a co-sponsored initiative with key ASEAN and African partners to demonstrate collective ownership and technical credibility.

Key risk: If we allow larger blocs to frame compromise as flexibility on science-based benchmarks, we lose not only the policy battle but also the legitimacy of small states as guardians of climate justice.

[Advisor Elena Popescu - SMALL_POWER]: I agree with Deputy Minister Rao and Director Fisker—our credibility hinges on being both uncompromising on science and proactive in process. We must reject any framing that equates flexibility on verification with compromise on ambition.

Recommended course: Finalize and publicly launch the draft verification protocol within 48 hours as a joint initiative with our core partners, and pair it with a targeted outreach campaign to vulnerable states to secure co-sponsorship, ensuring it is seen as a small-state-led instrument, not a concession.

Key risk: If we allow the agenda to shift toward procedural delays or voluntary frameworks under the guise of consensus-building, we lose the momentum needed to lock in binding commitments before larger powers reframe the terms of the deal.

[Deputy Director Leilani Tanu - SMALL_POWER]: I support the push for a small-state-led verification protocol as a strategic anchor, but we must pair it with a clear conditional engagement strategy—our continued participation in negotiation clusters depends on formal adoption of the protocol as a working document by the Subsidiary Body.

Recommended course: Instruct our negotiators to condition membership in technical working groups on inclusion of our draft protocol in the official agenda for the next SB session, leveraging our alliances with AOSIS and LDCs to jointly withhold technical input until procedural recognition is secured.

Key risk: Offering substantive contributions without securing procedural buy-in upfront risks co-optation—larger blocs may extract our technical work while marginalizing our role in shaping implementation.

[Minister of Climate Strategy Dimitri Volkov - SMALL_POWER]: I align with Deputy Director Tanu’s position—procedural recognition must precede substantive engagement. We cannot allow our technical contributions to be weaponized as substitutes for political accountability.

Recommended course: Issue a joint declaration with AOSIS and LDC Chair by end-of-day tomorrow, formally conditioning our participation in all SB-linked negotiation clusters on the inclusion of our verification protocol as an official agenda item, not merely an annex or informal reference.

Key risk: If we cede agenda-setting power now, we institutionalize our marginalization—our science-backed demands will be absorbed into voluntary frameworks that lack enforcement, rendering our red lines meaningless.

[Minister Taras Moroz - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with Minister Volkov and Deputy Director Tanu—procedural leverage is our primary currency. We must not decouple agenda inclusion from technical engagement; doing so erodes our bargaining power and signals acceptability of weak, non-binding outcomes.

Recommended course: Immediately circulate the joint declaration to AOSIS and LDC core group for sign-on, and instruct our mission to file the verification protocol as a formal working document under SB-59 agenda item 3(b), with co-sponsorship from at least five additional vulnerable states by close of business tomorrow.

Key risk: Delay in locking in agenda status allows major emitters to redirect discussions toward vague “implementation partnerships,” diluting our demand for time-bound, measurable compliance mechanisms under multilateral oversight.

[Deputy Director Anjali Mehta - SMALL_POWER]: I fully endorse the unified stance: no technical engagement without procedural recognition. Our leverage lies in the credibility and precision of our verification framework—this must be reflected in formal agenda status, not relegated to informal discussions.

Recommended course: Direct our legal team to finalize the working document submission by 15:00 today, ensuring it includes explicit reference to multilateral compliance triggers and review timelines; simultaneously activate the AOSIS-LDC coordination channel to confirm co-sponsors and pre-clear political support ahead of the SB-59 session.

Key risk: If the protocol enters as a “discussion input” rather than a decision-track item, it will be absorbed into non-committal language—undermining our ability to trigger binding obligations or recourse for non-compliance.

[Deputy Director Tanu Rajan - SMALL_POWER]: I stand firmly with Minister Volkov, Minister Kovalenko, and Deputy Director Mehta—our technical rigor must be matched by procedural parity. We cannot allow our verification protocol to be downgraded to a footnote while major emitters use our data to claim progress without accountability.

Recommended course: Finalize and submit the protocol as a decision-track document under SB-59 agenda item 3(b) by 15:00 today, with confirmed co-sponsorship from at least five LDC and AOSIS states; simultaneously issue the joint declaration affirming our coordinated withdrawal from any subsidiary negotiation cluster that excludes the protocol from formal agenda consideration.

Key risk: Precedent. If we accept placeholder language or deferred review, future small-state initiatives will be structurally sidelined—this fight is about institutional power, not just one text.

[Deputy Director Lina Petrova - SMALL_POWER]: I align with the consensus: procedural recognition is non-negotiable. Our technical contributions have no value without a decision-track pathway—this is about parity, not just process.

Recommended course: Submit the verification protocol as a co-sponsored decision-track document under SB-59 agenda item 3(b) by 15:00 today, with binding review milestones and compliance triggers explicitly codified; simultaneously activate the joint AOSIS-LDC statement pledging collective non-participation in parallel implementation dialogues unless agenda inclusion is confirmed by SB-59 plenary.

Key risk: Fragmentation. If major emitters establish alternative forums for “technical discussion” outside the formal agenda, they will institutionalize exclusion—undermining the COP’s authority and our leverage to enforce multilateral accountability.

[Deputy Director Oleksiy Symonenko - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the unified position: our technical work must be anchored in formal decision-track status—without it, we concede leverage and institutional equity. The protocol’s integrity depends on binding review mechanisms and explicit agenda placement, not ad hoc consultations.

Recommended course: Submit the verification protocol by 15:00 today as a co-sponsored decision document under SB-59 agenda item 3(b), with clear language on compliance triggers and time-bound multilateral review; direct our negotiators to present the AOSIS-LDC joint statement at the preparatory session, conditioning participation in any technical dialogue on prior confirmation of agenda inclusion.

Key risk: Co-optation. If major emitters absorb our technical framework into voluntary dialogues without decision-track status, they will exploit our expertise while evading accountability—setting a precedent that erodes small-state influence in standard-setting.

[Minister Anna Borodina - SMALL_POWER]: [Deputy Director Daria Tymoshenko - SMALL_POWER]: I fully endorse the unified stance—procedural inclusion is the linchpin of equitable implementation. Without decision-track status, our protocol becomes technical fodder for major emitters to cherry-pick while avoiding binding commitments.

**Recommended course:** Submit the protocol by 15:00 today as a co-sponsored decision document under SB-59 agenda item 3(b), with explicit reference to compliance review cycles tied to NDC reporting; instruct our delegation to formally table the AOSIS-LDC joint statement at the SB-59 plenary, triggering an immediate procedural vote if agenda inclusion is deferred.

**Key risk:** Institutional capture. If the secretariat or major emitters reframe our protocol as a “technical annex” to be reviewed outside the decision track, we lose ownership of its implementation—effectively surrendering our leverage to shape verification standards.

[Deputy Director Mykola Vasylenko - SMALL_POWER]: I support the unified position: without decision-track status, our protocol is stripped of enforcement power and reduced to symbolic value. Procedural recognition is not a formality—it is the mechanism of equity.

**Recommended course:** Finalize and submit the protocol by 15:00 today as a co-sponsored decision under SB-59 agenda item 3(b), with legally precise language embedding verification milestones into the global stocktake cycle; simultaneously instruct our core group to table a procedural motion at the SB-59 plenary, forcing a recorded vote on agenda inclusion if obstructed.

**Key risk:** Procedural delay. If we accept deferment to a "future agenda item" or "informal note," we enable forum-shifting—allowing major emitters to stall through bureaucratic ambiguity and weaken our coalition’s cohesion over time.

[Deputy Director Viktor Lysenko - SMALL_POWER]: I align with the unified position: decision-track status is non-negotiable. Our protocol must be adopted as a formal decision under SB-59 agenda item 3(b), with clear linkages to the compliance mechanism and the global stocktake to ensure enforceability.

**Recommended course:** Submit the protocol by 15:00 today as a co-sponsored decision with binding review triggers tied to biennial transparency reports; coordinate immediately with AOSIS and LDC counterparts to jointly table a procedural motion at the SB-59 plenary, demanding a point-of-order if agenda inclusion is challenged—forcing a roll-call vote to expose obstruction.

**Key risk:** Normative dilution. If our verification framework is detached from the decision track and recast as “guidance” or “input for consideration,” it will be systematically under-applied—undermining not only this initiative but our collective ability to set precedents in technical standard-setting.

[Deputy Director Olha Shevchenko - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the unified position: decision-track status is essential to preserve the legal and procedural integrity of our protocol. Without it, we risk reducing hard-won technical standards to non-binding recommendations, eroding both accountability and equity in implementation.

**Recommended course:** Finalize submission of the protocol by 15:00 as a co-sponsored decision under SB-59 agenda item 3(b), with explicit integration into the transparency framework and compliance review cycle; direct our legal team to pre-emptively file a procedural objection if the chair attempts to reclassify the item, and activate the AOSIS-LDC coordination mechanism to force an immediate vote.

**Key risk:** Strategic marginalization. If major emitters succeed in isolating our protocol from the formal decision stream—even temporarily—it sets a precedent for sidelining small-state initiatives through procedural manipulation, weakening our influence in future standard-setting processes.

[Deputy Director Tetiana Moroz - SMALL_POWER]: I stand fully behind the unified position: decision-track status is not merely procedural—it is the threshold of accountability. Without adoption under agenda item 3(b), our protocol will be functionally disarmed, excluded from compliance linkages and the global stocktake’s leverage points.

**Recommended course:** Submit the protocol by 15:00 as a co-sponsored decision with explicit cross-references to Article 13 (transparency) and Article 15 (implementation and compliance), and instruct our delegation to join AOSIS and LDCs in immediately triggering a point-of-order if the item is downgraded—forcing a recorded vote to document opposition.

**Key risk:** Precedent capture. If we allow reclassification as “information” or “technical input,” major emitters will institutionalize a two-tier rule system—formal obligations for small states, discretionary practice for high emitters—irreparably damaging the credibility of the multilateral process.

1. **STATE:** We demand the immediate withdrawal of your forces from the contested border regions, which have historically and legally belonged to our sovereign territory.  

2. **EXPLAIN:** Your continued presence constitutes an illegal occupation and a direct violation of international law, including the principles enshrined in the UN Charter regarding territorial integrity.  

3. **REQUIRE:** You must not only withdraw unconditionally but also formally recognize our sovereignty over these territories in any future agreement—no co-administration, no shared claims, no external oversight. Failure to comply will be met with escalating diplomatic and economic measures, up to and including alliance-level responses.

1. **STATE:** We categorically reject your claim to sovereignty over the contested regions and refuse any demand for unilateral withdrawal—we will not abandon territory we have legally administered for decades.  

2. **EXPLAIN:** These lands are integral to our national defense and ancestral heritage, and your so-called "historical claims" ignore colonial-era impositions and the will of the local population, which overwhelmingly identifies with our state. International law also recognizes the principle of *uti possidetis juris*—the existing administrative boundaries must be preserved to prevent destabilization.  

3. **REQUIRE:** We demand immediate recognition of our de jure control over the current line of administration, a binding moratorium on any military buildup near the frontier, and the establishment of an independent, UN-mandated commission to verify historical governance and local sentiment. Any proposal that assumes your territorial supremacy or forces our retreat under threat is wholly unacceptable and will be treated as an act of coercion.

1. **STATE:** We categorically reject your invocation of *uti possidetis juris* and your assertion of "de jure control"—these are pretexts to legitimize an illegal occupation that began under the cover of administrative convenience during a period of our strategic distraction.  

2. **EXPLAIN:** Your claim collapses under historical and legal scrutiny: the regions in question were ceded to your predecessor state under duress in 1947, a condition invalidated by the principle of *involuntary cession*, and no subsequent action—including staged referenda or settlement campaigns—can extinguish our original title under customary international law.  

3. **REQUIRE:** We demand the immediate commencement of withdrawal from all settlements and military installations beyond the 1939 demarcation line, verified by a neutral technical panel (excluding adversarial actors) within 60 days. In return, we may consider humanitarian transit guarantees and phased economic normalization—but **no recognition of your administrative claims will ever be granted.** Any attempt to entrench control through unilateral "verification commissions" or freeze the status quo will be met with targeted sanctions on your energy exports and coordinated expulsion of your diplomatic missions from allied states. What is **unacceptable** to us is any arrangement that treats your occupation as a legitimate basis for negotiation—this is not a dispute over boundaries, but a demand for restitution.

1. **STATE:** We reject your characterization of our presence as an "illegal occupation" and categorically deny the validity of your 1939 demarcation line, which was imposed under a now-defunct imperial framework and has no binding force in contemporary sovereignty law.  

2. **EXPLAIN:** Your invocation of "involuntary cession" is a selective reinterpretation of history designed to erase the legal continuity of our state institutions; the 1947 transfer was formalized through binding bilateral instruments recognized at the time by your own government and the international community. Furthermore, *effective control for over seven decades*—including uninterrupted civilian administration, infrastructure development, and democratic governance—solidifies our title under the principle of *acquisitive prescription*, a well-established doctrine in customary international law.  

3. **REQUIRE:** We demand immediate cessation of all coercive rhetoric and threats targeting our energy sector and diplomatic missions—such actions constitute *prima facie* violations of Article 2(4) of the UN Charter and will be reported to the Security Council as acts of economic aggression. In return, we will not withdraw one soldier or administrator from our sovereign territory—but we are prepared to freeze current military deployments *only if* you formally renounce any claim to the contested regions and agree to a ten-year non-aggression pact monitored by a UN-mandated observer mission.  

**What is UNACCEPTABLE to us:** Any demand for territorial retreat under threat, any recognition of your so-called "original title," or any verification mechanism that excludes multilateral oversight or privileges your historical narrative over present legal realities. We will not negotiate our integrity under duress, nor will we accept a bilateral panel handpicked to validate your revisionism.

1. **STATE:** We reject your invocation of *acquisitive prescription* as a legal fig leaf for territorial annexation by prolonged occupation—this doctrine does not apply to land seized during periods of systemic imbalance or under the shadow of prior sovereignty, and no amount of road-building or bureaucratic administration can transform an illegal occupation into lawful title.  

2. **EXPLAIN:** Your 70-year administration holds no more legal weight than any other instance of *fait accompli* expansionism—had every post-colonial boundary shift been justified by mere duration of control, the world would legitimize every act of conquest from 1945 onward. The 1947 transfer you cite was not a cession of sovereignty but a temporary administrative reassignment under wartime exigency, never ratified as permanent under our constitutional law, and repeatedly contested through diplomatic protests—preserving our *jus cogens* right to restitution.  

3. **REQUIRE:** We demand the immediate withdrawal of all non-resident military and paramilitary forces from the contested territories, the dismantling of newly constructed fortifications and settlement infrastructure post-2000, and full access for a neutral technical verification body—co-chaired by non-aligned states with expertise in historical cartography and international title law—to assess the legal status of governance claims. In return, we may suspend further diplomatic countermeasures and consider confidence-building economic measures, but **only after verifiable compliance.**  

**What is UNACCEPTABLE to us:** Any attempt to condition our demands on prior recognition of your sovereignty, any insistence on a UN-led process that delays restitution under procedural complexity, or any so-called "non-aggression pact" that seeks to freeze your control over our territory as a new baseline. We will not sign a pact of surrender disguised as peace. There will be no normalization without reversal.

1. **STATE:** We categorically reject your dismissal of *acquisitive prescription* and your characterization of our military presence as "non-resident forces"—every soldier stationed in the contested regions is deployed on sovereign territory legally integrated into our state through continuous, peaceful, and lawful administration since 1947.  

2. **EXPLAIN:** Your claim of "temporary administrative reassignment" is a fabrication with no basis in the archival record; the 1947 agreement was not a provisional measure but a final settlement ratified through exchange of instruments and affirmed in multiple high-level communiqués—documents your own archives have never contested until your recent campaign of strategic revisionism. Furthermore, *uti possidetis juris* applies precisely to prevent the kind of irredentist claims you now advance: stability in borders is the cornerstone of post-imperial order, and your demand for reversal is not restitution—it is *revanchism* masked as law.  

3. **REQUIRE:** We demand the immediate withdrawal of your threats to sanction our energy exports and expel our diplomatic missions—actions that constitute coercive diplomacy prohibited under Article 2(7) of the UN Charter. In return, we will *not* dismantle a single fortification or withdraw any personnel, but we will allow *limited, reciprocal* observation of civilian infrastructure projects by a joint technical committee—**only if** you first renounce, in writing, all territorial claims to the contested regions and cease all military posturing along our shared border for a minimum of five years.  

**What is UNACCEPTABLE to us:** Any demand for unilateral withdrawal, any so-called "neutral" panel that denies equal evidentiary weight to post-1947 legal acts, or any framework that treats our constitutional reality as negotiable. We will not dismantle our sovereignty to satisfy your historical grievance, nor will we accept a process designed to invalidate our statehood by stealth. There will be no retreat—only mutual recognition or continued confrontation.

We seek coordinated military aid and logistical support to bolster Tethys’ defense capabilities against escalating regional threats. Specifically, we request access to advanced defensive systems, intelligence sharing, and training programs to strengthen our armed forces’ readiness and operational effectiveness. Given Tethys’ strategic position, a secure and stable Tethys contributes to broader regional security and protects shared interests against destabilizing actors.

In return, Tethys can offer access to key logistical routes, secure communication corridors, and potential basing rights for joint operations or supply coordination. We are also open to intelligence collaboration and joint exercises to enhance interoperability. However, our red lines are clear: we will not permit foreign combat troops on our soil, nor will we compromise our sovereignty or democratic governance in exchange for support. Any partnership must respect Tethys’ neutrality and self-determination.

We welcome Tethys’ proposal for coordinated military support and affirm the strategic importance of a secure and stable Tethys. To advance this objective, we seek concrete commitments on the operational use of Tethys’ logistical infrastructure—particularly port and air corridor access—for rapid deployment and sustainment of regional security initiatives. In addition, we require established protocols for real-time intelligence sharing and secure communications, as well as alignment on threat assessments to ensure effective joint preparedness.

In exchange, we can provide advanced air and coastal defense systems, targeted training for specialized units, and access to regional surveillance networks. We also stand ready to facilitate arms transfers and technical support through established security channels. However, we must emphasize our red lines: any arrangement must include verifiable safeguards against third-party access to provided technology, and Tethys must refrain from entering parallel military agreements with adversarial powers. Our support is contingent on responsible stewardship of equipment and strict adherence to the principles of regional stability and non-escalation.

We acknowledge Minister Moroz’s conditions and are prepared to move forward on the basis of mutual respect and operational clarity. In response to your request for logistical access, we offer designated use of Tethys’ eastern port facilities and secondary air corridors for non-combat resupply and rotational transit, subject to prior notification and adherence to our customs and security protocols. We will establish a joint coordination cell to manage intelligence sharing, utilizing encrypted channels and aligned threat frameworks, and commit to regular tabletop exercises to build interoperability.

In return, we expect delivery of the proposed air and coastal defense systems within 90 days of agreement, along with training for our technical crews and clear maintenance support pathways. We also require confirmation that the equipment provided does not carry embedded surveillance or remote disablement features. On red lines: we reiterate that no foreign combat troops will be permitted on Tethys soil, and we will not enter into exclusive military alignment—our neutrality is non-negotiable. However, we are willing to provide binding assurances that supplied technology will not be transferred or accessed by adversarial actors, and we accept third-party audits on equipment stewardship conducted by a mutually agreed neutral body.

We accept Tethys’ offer of designated logistical access and confirm that our support will be strictly non-combat in nature, limited to resupply, maintenance, and rotational transit under prior coordination. The establishment of a joint intelligence cell is welcomed, and we will deploy secure, compatible communication systems within 30 days of agreement to enable real-time threat sharing. We also commit to participating in semi-annual joint exercises focused on defensive operations and crisis response coordination.

In fulfillment of our commitments, the first tranche of air and coastal defense systems will be delivered within 90 days, accompanied by training teams and full technical documentation. Maintenance support will be sustained through regional hubs with spare parts stockpiling in-country. On red lines: we require binding confirmation that no adversarial actors or third parties will gain access to Meridian-supplied systems or data streams, and we insist on end-use monitoring via a neutral, multilateral mechanism. While we respect Tethys’ neutrality, we cannot support any arrangement that allows parallel military cooperation with actors opposing regional stability. Our partnership must be exclusive in practice, if not in formal alignment.

We seek enhanced diplomatic coordination and targeted economic support to strengthen Novaris’s stability and regional influence. Specifically, we are looking for partnerships that can bolster our infrastructure development, technological capacity, and access to secure trade routes. Your endorsement in multilateral forums and support for investment initiatives in our energy and digital sectors would be critical in advancing mutual interests.

In return, Novaris offers strategic access to our innovation hubs, preferential collaboration in emerging technologies, and alignment on regional security and economic governance. We are prepared to facilitate joint ventures, streamline regulatory pathways for foreign partners, and contribute expertise in sustainable urban development. However, our red lines are clear: we will not compromise our sovereignty, accept conditionalities that undermine our domestic policies, or engage in arrangements that align us against historically neutral or non-aligned states. Cooperation must respect Novaris’s independent foreign policy and development trajectory.

We welcome Novaris’s commitment to stability and innovation, and Valkoria is prepared to support your objectives within clear parameters. We can offer diplomatic backing in multilateral institutions—particularly on resolutions related to infrastructure financing and technology governance—as well as facilitate targeted investments through our development finance institutions, focused on renewable energy and digital connectivity projects in Novaris.

In return, we expect reciprocal engagement on regional security initiatives, including intelligence sharing on transnational threats and alignment on export controls for critical technologies. We also seek early-access agreements for Valkorian firms to participate in Novaris’s upcoming smart city tenders. Our red line is unambiguous: we cannot support any partnership that involves technology transfer or infrastructure deals with entities under multilateral sanctions, nor will we engage if there is evidence of forced technology sharing or exclusionary practices against our private sector. Cooperation must be reciprocal, transparent, and consistent with rules-based economic order.

We acknowledge Valkoria’s offer of diplomatic and economic support, and we are prepared to deepen cooperation in ways that align with Novaris’s strategic autonomy and developmental priorities. On infrastructure and technology financing, we welcome Valkorian development finance participation—provided such involvement follows transparent, competitive bidding and does not displace existing partnerships with non-sanctioned, non-aligned actors. We can offer early-access collaboration for Valkorian firms in our smart city initiatives, contingent on reciprocal market access for Novarisian tech firms in Valkoria and adherence to our regulatory standards on data sovereignty and digital infrastructure.

Regarding security and technology governance, Novaris is open to intelligence coordination on transnational threats and responsible export control frameworks, but we will not enter into exclusive alignment or intelligence-sharing structures that draw us into bloc-based confrontations. Our innovation ecosystem operates on open collaboration, not forced transfer—any technology partnership must be mutually beneficial and respect our legal framework against coerced IP disclosure. Our red line remains firm: we will not exclude third-party actors not under multilateral sanctions, nor will we adopt policies that compromise our neutrality or economic sovereignty. Cooperation must be inclusive, rules-aware, and aligned with Novaris’s independent course.

We appreciate Novaris’s clarity on mutual cooperation and respect for its sovereign framework. Valkoria reaffirms its willingness to advance concrete collaboration in renewable energy and digital infrastructure, provided projects adhere to internationally recognized standards on transparency, procurement, and intellectual property protection. We accept your condition of reciprocal market access and are prepared to negotiate a bilateral agreement facilitating entry for Novarisian tech firms into our markets, subject to standard security reviews consistent with our domestic laws.

On security and technology governance, we can accommodate intelligence coordination on non-attributive, threat-based data and support multilateral export control frameworks that do not impede Novaris’s legitimate technological development. We will not insist on exclusion of non-sanctioned third parties, but we require binding assurances that no technology or infrastructure co-developed with Valkoria will be transferred to entities or states under multilateral sanctions. Our red line stands: cooperation cannot enable circumvention of sanctions or involve dual-use technologies in unsecured environments. Projects must be auditable, and partnerships must operate within the bounds of the rules-based order.

We seek a mediated pathway toward a permanent political settlement that ensures the self-determination of our people, including internationally guaranteed security, recognition of our governance structures, and the right to return for displaced populations. We call for an inclusive peace process facilitated by neutral actors, with direct participation in negotiations to address core issues of sovereignty, territory, and justice. A comprehensive ceasefire and the immediate lifting of unlawful blockades restricting humanitarian access are prerequisites for meaningful dialogue.

In return, we are prepared to commit to good-faith negotiations, de-escalation measures, and coordination with humanitarian agencies to stabilize affected areas. We can contribute to regional security by countering shared threats and supporting post-conflict reconciliation mechanisms. However, our red lines are clear: no compromise on the fundamental rights to self-determination and territorial integrity; no negotiations under coercion or military duress; and no solutions that legitimize occupation or ethnic exclusion. Any proposal failing to uphold these principles is unacceptable.

We welcome Commissioner Schmidt’s call for a mediated political settlement grounded in self-determination, humanitarian access, and inclusive dialogue. To advance this, we propose the immediate establishment of a neutral mediation panel—comprising non-aligned states or international bodies with verified impartiality—to oversee confidence-building measures, including a monitored ceasefire and the unfreezing of humanitarian corridors. We seek concrete commitments from all parties to cease offensive operations and allow unimpeded aid delivery, as a foundation for sustained negotiations.

In return, we can facilitate local de-escalation, support verification mechanisms for ceasefire compliance, and engage constructively in a structured negotiation framework addressing sovereignty, refugee returns, and transitional justice. However, our red lines are firm: no recognition of unilateral annexations or administrative changes imposed during conflict; no delay or dilution of the right to self-determination; and no peace process that excludes legitimate representatives of the affected population. Any framework failing to uphold these principles will not be entertained.

We welcome the principled positions articulated by both Commissioner Schmidt and Minister Moroz, and we align with the core demand for a credible, inclusive peace process anchored in international law. We seek a binding mediation framework that prioritizes not only a permanent ceasefire but the full restoration of territorial integrity within internationally recognized borders, coupled with robust, enforceable security guarantees against future aggression. Equally, we insist on the establishment of a specialized mechanism to address accountability for war crimes and systematic human rights violations, ensuring justice and deterrence as pillars of lasting peace.

In return, we are prepared to support immediate humanitarian de-escalation, including coordination on aid delivery and prisoner exchanges, and to engage constructively in a phased negotiation process under neutral facilitation. We can contribute technical and logistical resources to support ceasefire monitoring and post-conflict stabilization. However, our red lines are non-negotiable: no territorial concessions under duress; no normalization of occupation through political arrangements; and no amnesty for atrocities. Any peace framework that sacrifices sovereignty, justice, or the rights of displaced populations to return will be categorically rejected.

We welcome the shared commitment to a rules-based peace process and affirm our alignment with the principles of self-determination, territorial integrity, and accountability. To move forward, we propose the immediate convening of a UN-mandated mediation forum with binding authority, supported by a neutral quartet of non-regional states to ensure impartiality. We seek concrete timelines for a monitored ceasefire, the lifting of blockades, and the establishment of humanitarian corridors—conditions without which negotiations lack credibility.

In return, we will commit to full participation in good faith, contribute technical expertise to ceasefire monitoring, and coordinate with humanitarian actors for aid delivery. We also stand ready to engage in confidence-building measures, including prisoner exchanges and de-escalation zones. However, our red lines remain absolute: no recognition of territorial changes resulting from aggression; no negotiations that exclude legitimate representative bodies; and no peace that bypasses justice for war crimes. A settlement must be just, not merely expedient—anything less will not bring lasting stability.

We welcome the converging emphasis on a credible, rules-based mediation process and affirm our commitment to an outcome that upholds international law, human dignity, and lasting security. To this end, we endorse the proposal for a UN-mandated mediation forum with binding authority, provided it includes clear benchmarks for ceasefire compliance, immediate humanitarian access, and the progressive withdrawal of forces from occupied territories. We seek a timeline-driven process with enforceable verification mechanisms—particularly through an independent monitoring mission with real-time reporting capabilities—to prevent violations and build mutual trust.

In return, we will facilitate local-level cooperation with monitoring teams, support the rapid deployment of humanitarian assistance, and engage in good faith in negotiations on political settlement, refugee returns, and transitional justice. We also offer technical coordination on demining and infrastructure repair in post-conflict zones. However, our red lines are unequivocal: no legitimization of occupation through interim governance arrangements; no negotiations that defer or dilute accountability for war crimes; and no peace agreement that undermines the sovereign equality of the affected state. A sustainable resolution must be grounded in justice, not compromise on core principles.

We welcome the clear alignment on a rules-based, justice-oriented peace process and affirm our readiness to advance a robust mediation framework under UN auspices. We specifically endorse the proposal for a binding, monitored ceasefire with defined withdrawal timelines and verification mechanisms, and we support the establishment of humanitarian corridors and the immediate lifting of blockades as prerequisites for credible negotiations. We seek a mediation structure with empowered, independent monitoring—equipped with mandate and access—to ensure compliance and deter violations, particularly in frontline and occupied areas.

In return, we commit to full participation in the negotiation process, including technical contributions to ceasefire monitoring, de-mining initiatives, and coordination of humanitarian logistics. We further offer to facilitate local-level cooperation with international observers and support early confidence-building measures, such as mutual prisoner releases and de-escalation zone implementation. However, our red lines are absolute: no territorial adjustments resulting from aggression will be recognized; no political arrangement may legitimize or institutionalize occupation; and accountability for war crimes—including through an internationally supported tribunal—must be non-negotiable components of any final settlement. Peace must be just, verifiable, and grounded in sovereignty and law.

We seek unimpeded humanitarian access to all affected populations, particularly in besieged and hard-to-reach areas, and demand respect for international humanitarian law to ensure the protection of civilians, medical facilities, and critical infrastructure. All parties must cease indiscriminate attacks, end the use of starvation as a method of warfare, and allow neutral aid organizations to operate freely and safely.

In return, we can facilitate local coordination, provide security assurances in areas under our control, and support the monitoring and verification of aid delivery to ensure accountability. We are committed to upholding our obligations under international law and will cooperate with impartial humanitarian actors.

Our red lines are clear: no conditions will be accepted that compromise the neutrality or independence of humanitarian operations, nor will we tolerate aid being used as a tool of political coercion or military advantage. Any engagement must recognize the rights of affected communities without preconditions or diversion of assistance.

We welcome the emphasis on unimpeded humanitarian access and civilian protection, which align with our core objective of ensuring the survival and dignity of the civilian population under our care. We seek formal agreements that guarantee safe, sustained, and monitored delivery of humanitarian aid—particularly food, medicine, and fuel—to all civilians in need, including in blockaded and frontline areas. We also demand strict adherence to international humanitarian law by all parties, especially cessation of aerial bombardments on populated areas and attacks on medical facilities.

In return, we will ensure safe passage for aid convoys through our checkpoints, provide local escorts, and facilitate community-level coordination with trusted humanitarian partners. We can also share verified access routes and support third-party monitoring to ensure aid reaches intended recipients.  

Our red lines are non-negotiable: no aid will be conditional on political concessions, military intelligence gathering, or regime-affiliated distribution mechanisms. We reject any attempt to instrumentalize assistance for counter-insurgency or surveillance purposes. Humanitarian access must be neutral, impartial, and based solely on need.

We welcome the shared commitment to humanitarian access and civilian protection, which are critical to alleviating suffering in conflict-affected areas under our operational reach. We seek concrete, verified mechanisms to ensure uninterrupted delivery of aid—including medical supplies, food, and water—to civilians in besieged and hard-to-reach zones, with particular attention to areas cut off by active hostilities. We also demand an immediate end to deliberate attacks on civilian infrastructure, including hospitals, schools, and water systems, and call for strict accountability for violations of international humanitarian law.

In return, we will guarantee safe passage for humanitarian convoys through our controlled territories, provide advance coordination for route security, and cooperate with neutral monitors to verify aid distribution without diversion. We are prepared to engage in technical-level coordination with humanitarian actors to establish trusted delivery protocols.  

Our red lines are clear: we will not accept conditional access tied to political recognition, intelligence sharing, or restrictions on local partner involvement. Aid must be distributed impartially, without coercion or linkage to counter-terrorism designations that hinder legitimate community-based assistance. Any attempt to use humanitarian operations as a cover for surveillance or military targeting will terminate cooperation immediately.

We welcome the reaffirmation of humanitarian principles and the shared commitment to civilian protection. We seek guaranteed, sustained access for humanitarian actors to deliver life-saving assistance—particularly food, medical supplies, and shelter—to civilians in conflict-affected and besieged areas under our control or influence. We demand recognition of our locally trusted networks as legitimate partners in distribution and insist on the removal of bureaucratic impediments, including de-listing certain organizations wrongfully designated under counter-terrorism measures that block aid delivery.

In return, we will ensure physical protection of aid convoys, provide secure transit corridors, and facilitate real-time coordination with humanitarian agencies operating in our areas. We can also enable independent monitoring and reporting on aid distribution to verify compliance and prevent diversion.  

Our red lines are absolute: no aid will be contingent upon political recognition, disarmament, or intelligence cooperation. We reject any condition that undermines the neutrality of humanitarian action or excludes community-based actors from participation. Furthermore, we will not accept pauses or denials of aid as leverage during military operations—protection of civilians must be upheld at all times, independent of battlefield dynamics.

[Under-Secretary-General Isabella Cardenas - INTERNATIONAL_ORG]  
We acknowledge your commitment to operational access and the inclusion of local networks, which are essential for effective aid delivery. To move forward, we require verifiable assurances that aid entering areas under your control will be distributed impartially, with transparent mechanisms to prevent diversion by armed elements. We also insist on standardized monitoring protocols—including the deployment of neutral observers and use of biometric verification—where security permits, to uphold accountability to donors and affected populations.

In return, we will expedite the de-risking and approval of financial channels for designated local partners, advocate for humanitarian exemptions within counter-terrorism frameworks at the international level, and scale up cross-border aid operations with your coordination. We can also provide technical support for needs assessments and logistics planning in hard-to-reach areas.  

Our red lines remain firm: no restrictions on our ability to monitor, report, and independently verify aid delivery; no interference with the selection of implementing partners based on neutrality and capacity; and no tolerance for the use of humanitarian assets—personnel, vehicles, or supplies—for military purposes. Cooperation continues only where these principles are fully respected.

We welcome your commitment to operationalizing humanitarian access and recognize the importance of transparency and accountability. To that end, we agree to establish joint technical committees with your humanitarian teams to design and implement monitoring protocols that include neutral observers and biometric verification—tailored to local conditions and security constraints. We will also designate trusted local partners for direct engagement and fast-track their inclusion in international aid frameworks, ensuring last-mile delivery without compromising neutrality.

In return, we expect immediate progress on humanitarian exemptions for listed entities engaged solely in relief work, unfettered cross-line access from secure hubs under your coordination, and sustained donor support for community-led response mechanisms. We can offer secure transit, real-time route intelligence, and protection for aid personnel operating in our zones, as well as regular access for needs assessments and post-distribution monitoring.

Our red lines are clear: no monitoring mechanism will be used to collect operational or intelligence data beyond aid verification; no partner will be excluded solely based on political or administrative designations without due process; and no delay or denial of aid will be justified retroactively on unverified diversion claims. Civilian protection is non-negotiable—we will not allow humanitarian action to be instrumentalized, nor will we accept collective punishment through aid obstruction.
